title: Tomato Harvest Pricing Run Seed 42-3
scenario: Tomato Ketchup in California. Supplier-to-manufacturer pricing sets the manufacturer cost basis before the retailer negotiation.

product_context.demand_signal: Retail ketchup demand is steady, but wholesale buyers want price protection before peak tomato procurement.
product_context.supply_signal: Tomato harvest yields are uneven across growing regions, putting pressure on paste input pricing.

agents.name: Supplier
agents.objective: Sell above the supplier reservation price while keeping volume committed.

agents.name: Manufacturer
agents.objective: Buy below the procurement ceiling and preserve downstream margin.

agents.name: Retailer
agents.objective: Buy enough units without exceeding the retail reservation price.

phases.name: supplier_manufacturer
phases.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
phases.description: The supplier negotiates an upstream unit price with the manufacturer.

phases.name: manufacturer_retailer
phases.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
phases.description: The manufacturer negotiates a downstream unit price with the retailer after the upstream deal sets its cost basis.

steps.message: Thanks for opening the conversation. Based on current market levels, I can start at 6.90 per unit for a committed volume, which reflects the prevailing price environment and gives us a workable supply position. If your volume and scheduling are firm, I’d be open to discussing structure and potential movement from there. This is the opening turn with no buyer offer yet, so the reasonable move is to anchor near the latest observed market reference of 6.50 while leaving room to negotiate. The offer stays well above the supplier’s internal floor and signals willingness to trade if the buyer can commit on volume.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for supplier_manufacturer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 6.50.

steps.message: Thanks for the opening, but 6.90 is far outside a range I can work with on the manufacturing side. Even allowing for a firm program and clean scheduling, my economics are anchored much lower, so the most I can put forward to start is 2.35 per unit. If you have flexibility tied to volume commitment or delivery structure, we can explore that, but I’d need to see a very substantial move from your side to keep this actionable. The supplier opened at 6.90, while the buyer has not yet made an offer. A low counteroffer is a reasonable response to signal a large valuation gap, preserve margin, and leave room to negotiate without ending the discussion.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.35, below its hidden seller floor of 2.98.

The offer stays well above the supplier’s internal floor and signals willingness to trade if the buyer can commit on volume.

steps.message: I appreciate the candid starting point, but 2.35 is below where I can responsibly supply, even with a structured program. The broader market is supporting meaningfully higher levels, so I need to move toward a realistic commercial range rather than chase an uneconomic number. I can come down from 6.90 to 5.80 per unit as a serious concession, and if you can pair that with committed volume and stable scheduling, we can keep narrowing the gap. The buyer's offer is just under my internal floor, so accepting it would not make sense. At the same time, walking away this early is unnecessary, especially with a strong market reference and room to test whether the buyer has flexibility if I make a meaningful but controlled concession.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement, but 5.80 is still far outside a range I can responsibly carry into my downstream business. I do recognize you're signaling seriousness, so I'll move off my opening position to 2.48 per unit as a good-faith step. If there's room for a much sharper adjustment on your side, especially tied to steadier volume and scheduling, we can keep working toward something commercial. Their offer remains extremely far from a workable buy level, but ending the discussion now is premature. A modest increase from 2.35 to 2.48 shows engagement without giving up too much margin or anchoring too high against a seller citing a strong market.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.48, below its hidden seller floor of 2.98.

steps.message: I appreciate the good-faith move to 2.48, but that level is still well below where this business clears on my side, especially against the current market backdrop and the need to commit supply with confidence. I can make a meaningful concession from 5.80 to 5.10 per unit if we are talking about reliable volume and clean scheduling. If you can narrow the gap materially from here, I’m prepared to keep working rather than let this stall. The buyer moved up only modestly, while the latest market check is very strong relative to both offers. A moderate concession keeps the negotiation alive, signals flexibility, and still protects the supplier’s position above reservation while testing whether the buyer is serious.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

I can improve from 2.48 to 2.60 per unit if we are discussing dependable volume and straightforward scheduling on our side. That is a real concession from us and about as far as I can responsibly stretch today, so if you can come substantially closer, we may still have a path. The supplier remains far above the buyer’s workable range, but rejecting now would end the discussion prematurely. A modest increase keeps the negotiation alive, signals limited flexibility, and tests whether the supplier is serious about narrowing the gap despite the strong market reference.

The latest market reference we are seeing remains very strong, so I can make a further concession only to 4.65 per unit if this comes with dependable volume and clean scheduling. The buyer's offer is above our floor but far below a reasonable negotiated outcome, especially relative to the current market reference of 10.30. With limited rounds remaining, a measured concession tests whether the buyer can move materially while preserving margin and signaling seriousness.

steps.message: I recognize you've moved meaningfully from your opening position, but 4.65 is still well outside a range we can make work on our side given our manufacturing economics and downstream commitments. The latest market read we have supports some movement, so I can improve to 2.90 per unit if we can keep the volume dependable and execution straightforward. That is a real step up from 2.60 and is intended to show seriousness while still keeping the program commercially viable for us. Their offer remains far above our workable economics, but there is still time for one more constructive move. A modest increase signals engagement and tests whether the supplier is prepared to close the gap materially without us giving away too much margin.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 2.90, but that level is still too far from where we can responsibly commit supply, especially with volume reliability and execution certainty in view. The latest market reference we are seeing remains materially stronger, so I can make a meaningful concession to 4.10 per unit if we lock in dependable volume and keep the program straightforward to execute. That narrows the gap substantially from my last position and is intended as a practical closing move rather than a placeholder. Their offer is above our floor but leaves substantial room, and the market check is very favorable to the supplier side. With only one turn left, a firmer but still concessional counter is more reasonable than accepting 2.90 or walking away.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: I recognize the market is supporting stronger pricing right now, and I appreciate your move down to 4.10. That said, we still have to make the economics work on our side, so I can improve from 2.90 to 3.15 per unit if we can close cleanly now with dependable volume and straightforward execution. This is a serious end-of-process position intended to narrow the gap materially without putting the program upside down for us. The supplier’s 4.10 offer is well above our workable buy range, but rejecting outright on the final turn gives up any chance of a close. A modest but meaningful increase from 2.90 to 3.15 signals flexibility while still protecting downstream margin as much as possible despite the strong market reference.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 3.15, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.63.

negotiations.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
negotiations.outcome_summary: Supplier to Manufacturer reached the turn limit without agreement.

diagnosis.outcome: Upstream deal timeout. Downstream negotiation did not run because the upstream deal did not close.
diagnosis.chain_effect: The upstream deal did not establish a downstream cost basis, so phase two was skipped.
key_risks: A costly upstream agreement compresses downstream negotiating room.
Turn limits can force timeouts even when the gap is narrowing.
suggested_next_actions: Tune reservation prices and turn limits to test sensitivity.
Add richer quantity or margin tradeoffs before introducing LLM behavior.

notes: Backend simulation with linked upstream and downstream negotiations.
